---
title: "7. Research Builds on Research"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb02_research_builds_on_research_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** Given a source an AI tool or a search hands you, you
> decide whether it counts as *verified* (retrieved and read by you). And given the
> sources you have verified, you decide whether the gap you want to study is
> *genuine* or is only the place where your search happened to stop.

## Why this decision matters

Picture the moment a **thesis advisor** hands your first draft back with one line
circled: *"You cite six papers. Which did you actually read, and where is the hole you
claim to fill?"* That question is the whole job. A literature built from
confident-sounding summaries collapses the instant a reviewer opens one citation and
finds it says something else, or finds it does not exist. This chapter's decision turns
"I looked it up" into "I retrieved it, I read it, and I can show you the empty spot on
the map."

## The concept

A field is a **cumulative conversation**: a running argument where each study answers,
extends, or challenges the ones before it. One lab measures a reaction rate, a second
repeats it with a new catalyst, a third pools both. Your job is not to invent an island.
It is to find the conversation your question belongs to and add one honest sentence.

You run one habit on every source. The **retrieval-verification loop** has four steps:
ask a tool for leads, retrieve the source yourself, verify it exists and says what was
claimed, and document where you found it. A **lead** is a candidate you have not checked
yet, such as a citation an AI hands you. It stays a lead until you retrieve and read it.

From one verified source you grow the rest by **snowballing**: walking its links two
ways. **Backward citation search** reads the source's own reference list for older work
it built on. **Forward citation search** finds the newer papers that cite it. Two solid
sources, snowballed both ways, quickly become ten.

Snowballing lets you tell a real gap from a dead end. A **genuine gap** is a question
your verified sources actually leave unanswered, confirmed by looking both ways. An
**unfinished search** is one you simply stopped chasing. They feel identical from your
desk, and only one is a contribution.

You then arrange what you found as an **evidence map**: each verified source becomes one
**claim node** (what it asserts, in one sentence), lines connect claims that agree or
contradict, and one marked spot shows where no source reaches. That spot is your gap,
honest only because every node around it is a source you opened.

## A worked example

Say your curiosity is green chemistry. You have read that **silver nanoparticles**
(specks of silver billionths of a meter across) can be made without harsh reagents by
reducing silver ions with a mild acid. Your question: does **L-ascorbic acid** (ordinary
vitamin C) reduce silver ions to nanoparticles at room temperature, and does more of it
make the particles smaller?

You ask an AI for the key studies. It returns a confident citation: a plausible author
pair, a 2019 date, a journal called *Journal of Green Chemistry*. You go to read it, and
the exact title returns nothing in your library portal, nothing on Google Scholar,
nothing on the publisher's site. The journal does not exist; the real Royal Society of
Chemistry title is simply *Green Chemistry*. The citation was fabricated.

So you run the loop properly. You retrieve one real review of green nanoparticle
synthesis, read it, and snowball. Its references point backward to the reduction
chemistry it built on; its "Cited by" list points forward to recent ascorbic-acid
studies. Within an hour your map holds a half-dozen real sources and a contradiction:
one paper reports that more reductant yields smaller particles, another the opposite.
That disagreement is the live edge of the field, and it tells you the question is
unsettled.

Now the decision that is yours. The broad question "can vitamin C make silver
nanoparticles" is no gap: the field is enormous. But your narrow question, how particle
size behaves across a specific concentration range where two real papers already
disagree, may be genuine. You can defend it only because you retrieved the sources around
it and they still leave it open.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb02 — Research builds on research: verified evidence +
real gaps**. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this
chapter. In the lab you watch a pile of fifteen AI citations collapse to the few that
survive retrieval, build an evidence map in code, and run the retrieval-verification loop
on your own claim.

## Recommended AI prompts

Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt earns a better draft; none earns
your trust.

**Locate.** Ask for leads to chase, not a finished bibliography.

```text
Act as a literature scout in [your field]. My question is: [paste it].
List the five published studies most relevant to it, each with authors,
year, exact title, and venue, so I can retrieve and verify each myself.
Only include work you are confident exists; mark anything uncertain.
```

*After running, verify:* retrieve every citation by searching its exact title in quotes
before you believe it exists. Counters **confident fabrication** (a real-sounding
citation for a paper that was never written).

**List-to-verify (snowball).** Start from one source you have already read.

```text
Here is one source I have verified: [authors, year, exact title, venue].
Name three older works from its reference list it likely built on, and
three newer works that likely cite it, each with authors, year, title,
and venue, plus one line on why it belongs on the chain.
```

*After running, verify:* cross-check the "backward" names against the seed's real
reference list and retrieve the rest. A tidy chain can quietly omit the one contradicting
study. Counters **illusion of completeness**.

**Red-team.** Make the tool attack your gap, not flatter it.

```text
Here is my question and the gap I placed: [one or two sentences].
Argue that this gap is NOT real. Name up to three published studies that
may already answer it, with authors, year, title, and venue, and say what
each would have to show to close it. Do not reassure me.
```

*After running, verify:* if it just agrees the gap is open and names nothing, push back
and make it try to close the gap; then retrieve every study it names. Counters
**sycophantic agreement**.

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
Two decisions never leave your hands. Whether a source counts as **verified**: only
reading it yourself settles that. And whether your **gap is real**: only retrieval that
comes up empty earns the word. What your project may claim follows from those two. An AI
can point; it cannot certify.
:::

## An AI failure case

The most common failure here is **citation hallucination**, the taxonomy's *confident
fabrication*: the model invents a source and presents it with full confidence. In the
worked example it gave you a plausible author pair, a 2019 date, and *Journal of Green
Chemistry*. You caught it in a minute: you searched the exact title in quotes across your
library portal and Google Scholar, found nothing, and noticed the journal does not exist.
Fluent confidence is not evidence. If you cannot locate a source where it should live,
treat it as fabricated until you prove otherwise.

## Verification laboratory

The key result of this chapter is a verdict: this source is verified, and this gap is (or
is not) real. Verify it with **primary-source reading** (verification guide, method 4):
find the actual source, open it, and read the line that supports the claim before you
cite it. Do this for every source on your map, not a sample. For a gap you will defend
out loud, add a second, independent check such as **peer reasoning**: walk your gap past
a classmate, whose one question often exposes a source you skipped. Two independent checks
that agree are far stronger than one.

## Project transfer

This chapter builds your **M2 verified evidence and contribution map**, the milestone you
present as a gallery walk at Friday's studio. It puts the question and guessed gap from
the previous chapter to the test of retrieval. Leave with four pieces drafted: a
verified-source log (author, year, where you retrieved it, its claim in your words), an
evidence map naming at least one contradiction, your gap placed honestly with its
honesty-test sentence, and a one-sentence contribution line that stops where your
retrieval stops. That map is the ground your later research charter stands on.

## Defend your decision

In one short paragraph, state your gap in a single sentence, then defend it. Name its
**compass position** (description or generalization, the kinds of claim this evidence work
supports). Say plainly whether the gap is confirmed by retrieval or is still an unfinished
search. Then name your uncertainty: how many sources you have actually opened, and what
your map cannot see. If you cannot yet write the honest boundary sentence, *"across the
sources I retrieved and verified, none addresses my gap,"* say what retrieval it still
owes. That is a finding, not a failure.
